The privacy policy served at `/privacy` explicitly states security/audit logs are kept for a limited period then discarded — a commitment the code does not fulfill. By contrast, `usageEventsRepository` and `aiUsageRepository` already implement `pruneBefore()` and are wired into a retention sweep, establishing the correct pattern.
findings.impact: In a long-running deployment, the sessions and audit_log tables become unbounded stores of IP addresses for every user who ever authenticated. Under GDPR/CCPA the gap between the stated policy and actual behavior is a compliance risk. The right-to-erasure pathway is also incomplete.
findings.recommendation: Add `pruneExpiredBefore(cutoffMs)` to `sessionsRepository` (delete where `expires_at <= cutoffMs`), `pruneBefore(cutoffMs)` to `auditRepository`, `codes.pruneConsumedOrExpiredBefore(cutoffMs)` and `tokens.pruneRevokedOrExpiredBefore(cutoffMs)` to `oauthRepository`, and `pruneRevokedOrConsumedBefore(cutoffMs)` to `guestRepository`. Wire all into the existing retention sweep with a configurable window (e.g. `CANVAS_DROP_AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS`). This closes the policy-vs-code gap and follows the pattern already in place for usage events.
alternatives: Update the privacy policy to state indefinite retention until the prune mechanism is built (reduces legal risk while deferring the work)
Add only the audit_log and sessions prune as an immediate fix, deferring oauth tokens and guest invites to a follow-up

findings.title: Bearer deploy API allows deploy and rollback on archived/disabled canvases
findings.problem: authCanvas() verifies only the API key match; it never checks canvas.status. The management API guards the identical operations with `if (cv.status !== 'active') return NOT_ACTIVE` (management.ts line 822). An agent holding a valid Bearer key can therefore push a new deploy or roll back a version on an archived or admin-disabled canvas. For an archived canvas this silently updates currentVersionId while the URL 404s for visitors; on unarchive the canvas will serve whatever the agent deployed while it was offline. For a disabled canvas the owner can update the live content pointer even after an admin takedown.
findings.impact: A canvas owner whose canvas was admin-disabled can silently change what it serves before re-enable. An archived canvas may surface unexpected content on unarchive, confusing both the owner and visitors.
findings.recommendation: After authCanvas() returns a valid canvas, add: `if (auth.status !== 'active') return c.json({ code: 'NOT_ACTIVE', message: 'Unarchive this canvas before deploying.' }, 409);` in PUT /:id/deploy and POST /:id/rollback, mirroring management.ts line 822. Apply the same guard inside upload.finalize() for the staged-upload path.
alternatives: Push the status check into authCanvas() itself so the guard is shared by all Bearer-keyed mutations automatically.
Add a regression test that archives a canvas and confirms PUT /:id/deploy returns 409; defer the code fix to the next maintenance window.

findings.title: KV increment accepts NaN/Infinity as the `by` delta
findings.problem: `typeof NaN === 'number'` and `typeof Infinity === 'number'` are both true, so the guard `typeof body.by === 'number'` accepts them. Infinity/NaN forwarded into SQL `CAST(value AS REAL) + by` produces NULL in SQLite (corrupting the counter silently) or an out-of-range error in Postgres that escapes the KvNotNumericError catch and surfaces as an unhandled 500.
findings.impact: Any canvas SDK caller can corrupt a shared KV counter to NULL (SQLite) or crash the handler with a 500 (Postgres), across both dialects.
findings.recommendation: Change line 127 to: `const by = typeof body.by === 'number' && Number.isFinite(body.by) ? body.by : 1;` and return a 400 when the body explicitly supplies a non-finite value rather than silently defaulting to 1. Alternatively, use `z.number().finite()` in a Zod schema consistent with other validated routes.
alternatives: Let the default-to-1 behavior stand for NaN/Infinity but add an explicit 400 rejection so callers get actionable feedback instead of a silent default.
Add a DB-layer guard in the KV repository's increment implementation.

findings.title: galleryTags has no per-tag or array-length cap in settingsSchema
findings.problem: `galleryTags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` has no element count limit and no per-tag string length limit. All other string fields in the same schema have explicit `.max()` limits (title: 200, description: 2000, gallerySummary: 500). An authenticated org member can PATCH settings with thousands of arbitrarily long tags; the value is stored as a JSON column and returned in every canvas list row, inflating all list responses and gallery facet queries.
findings.impact: A single owner can make every canvas list response in the dashboard and admin panel excessively large, degrading UX for all users of the instance.
findings.recommendation: Add bounds: `z.array(z.string().max(50)).max(20).optional()` (or tunable limits appropriate to the gallery UX). This is consistent with every other bounded field in the same schema.
alternatives: Apply limits only at the DB layer as a JSON column length check.
Cap at the service layer in a tags-normalizer function called before persistence.

findings.title: Files upload and draft file write buffer unbounded body before size enforcement
findings.problem: canvas-files.ts POST reads `await c.req.formData()` then `file.arrayBuffer()` with no Hono bodyLimit middleware, so the full multipart body is allocated in memory before FileTooLargeError can fire (at the service layer, after the bytes are in memory). draft-api.ts PUT /:id/draft/file has the same gap: `new Uint8Array(await c.req.arrayBuffer())` with no preceding bodyLimit. The deploy/management routes all carry `blobBodyLimit` or `deployBodyLimit` before their body reads; these two paths were missed. An authenticated org member or enabled guest can send a multi-GB body that exhausts server memory before rejection.
findings.impact: On a shared deployment an authenticated user can OOM the Node process, causing a service outage for all tenants.
findings.recommendation: Add `blobBodyLimit` middleware to both routes: `app.post('/', blobBodyLimit, async (c) => {...})` in canvas-files.ts and `app.put('/:id/draft/file', sameOrigin, blobBodyLimit, async (c) => {...})` in draft-api.ts. Both can import blobBodyLimit from deploy-common.ts, matching the pattern at management.ts line 924 for the folder-deploy route.
alternatives: Use a global body-size guard mounted in app.ts before all routes, removing the need to add per-route middleware.
Defer canvas-files.ts to next sprint; the draft API touches only the authenticated owner and is lower urgency.

findings.title: SDK response bypasses securityHeadersMiddleware — nosniff, Referrer-Policy, COOP absent
findings.problem: The handler returns `new Response(cached, { headers: {...} })` with manually constructed headers that do not call `baseSecurityHeaders()`. Because this is a self-built Response rather than `c.json()`/`c.text()`, Hono's securityHeadersMiddleware (which only sets `c.header()`) does not propagate into it. The SDK JavaScript served to all canvas pages is therefore missing `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `Referrer-Policy: same-origin`, and `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin`. The security-headers.ts docstring's claim that 'a new surface can't ship without them' is falsified by this handler. deploy-api.ts GET /:id/files (line 252) has the identical gap.
findings.impact: The SDK script response lacks `nosniff`, which allows older IE/Firefox to sniff the content type. More materially, the docstring guarantee is false, making future audits harder.
findings.recommendation: Build a `new Headers()`, call `baseSecurityHeaders(headers)`, then set content-type and cache-control on it before constructing the Response: mirrors the pattern in preview/screenshot handlers. Add a test in serve-sdk.test.ts asserting the response includes `x-content-type-options: nosniff`.
alternatives: Update the docstring to explicitly list the handler exceptions instead of claiming universal coverage.
Wrap the response in a Hono helper that merges the middleware-set headers automatically.

findings.title: AI chat messages and system prompt have no body-size or string-length cap
findings.problem: `messages[].content` and `system` are `z.string()` with no `.max()`. There is no bodyLimit middleware on the AI chat route. An authenticated canvas caller can send an arbitrarily large prompt that is fully buffered in memory and then forwarded to the upstream AI provider, bypassing the pre-call USD quota heuristic (which is based on spend, not token count). The `maxTokens` cap only limits output.
findings.impact: A canvas SDK caller can send an enormous input that allocates memory, generates large AI provider costs, and makes the USD quota guard ineffective (the spend from one huge request can exceed the quota without a pre-call check catching it).
findings.recommendation: Add a body-limit middleware to the AI chat route (e.g. 256 KB) and add Zod length caps: `content: z.string().max(65536)`, `system: z.string().max(16384).optional()`, and `messages: z.array(...).min(1).max(50)`.
alternatives: Estimate token count pre-call from the request body length and reject if it would exceed a token budget, rather than a raw byte limit.
Use the existing `deployBodyLimit` (25 MB) as a rough guard if a dedicated AI body limit is not feasible immediately.

findings.title: /api/me spread-leak test has a stale field count — canPublishPublic is silently missing from the assertion
findings.problem: The 'returns exactly the projected fields (no spread leak)' test asserts Object.keys(body) equals 8 fields, but me.ts line 46 returns 9 (canPublishPublic was added later). The stub at line 1303 omits canPublishPublic from the user object, so JSON serialization silently drops the undefined field and the assertion passes. The test therefore provides no safety against future accidental field additions or removals: a new internal column that is defined on the user object would also pass as long as the test stub doesn't define it.
findings.impact: The test that is supposed to be the contract guard for the /api/me projection is a false green. A future schema change that leaks a sensitive field (e.g. passwordHash) would pass this test if the stub doesn't happen to set it.
findings.recommendation: Add `canPublishPublic: true` to the stub (line 1303) and add `'canPublishPublic'` to the expected keys array at line 1320. Also assert `expect(body.canPublishPublic).toBe(true)` alongside the existing negatives. This turns the test into a genuine contract test for all 9 projected fields.
alternatives: Additionally assert that the response has exactly 9 keys with no others (the existing `.toEqual` already does this once the expected list is corrected).
Use a snapshot test for the full me-response shape.

findings.title: Admin cross-site rejection tested only for one route — all other admin mutations uncovered
findings.problem: Only PUT /api/admin/settings/models at test line 486 has an explicit Sec-Fetch-Site: cross-site rejection test. All other admin mutations — canvas disable/enable/restore, user block/unblock/promote/demote/grant-public/revoke-public, allowed-emails add/delete — use the `sameOriginPost` helper in happy-path tests, which passes the correct header. If requireSameOrigin were accidentally removed from any of these routes, the test suite would not detect it.
findings.impact: A regression removing the same-origin guard on an admin mutation (e.g. canvas disable or user block) would go undetected, enabling CSRF from a browser session.
findings.recommendation: Add a parameterized cross-site rejection test that iterates over every admin mutation route (method + path pairs) and asserts 403 when `Sec-Fetch-Site: cross-site` is sent.
alternatives: Add individual cross-site tests per route group rather than a parameterized sweep.
Add a shared test helper that wraps each admin mutation test and automatically adds a cross-site check.

findings.title: McpRoutesDeps and McpToolDeps duplicate 14 fields with no shared base type
findings.location: McpRoutesDeps interface lines 30-63; McpToolDeps in server.ts lines 46-71
findings.problem: Both interfaces declare the same 14 fields (config, users, canvases, versions, engine, upload, storage, guests, mailer, clone, drafts, usage, files, aiUsage) with matching types. McpRoutesDeps adds only the OAuth-layer extras. Because there is no shared base, the routes.ts handler manually reconstructs the McpToolDeps object field-by-field at lines 149-168. A field added to McpToolDeps but forgotten in the reconstruction is a TypeScript error, but a field added to McpRoutesDeps without threading it through to buildMcpServer silently drops it.
findings.impact: Adding a new dependency to McpToolDeps requires three edits (interface, McpRoutesDeps, and the reconstruction block); missing any one silently degrades the tool without a compile error on the omitted path.
findings.recommendation: Have McpRoutesDeps extend McpToolDeps: export interface McpRoutesDeps extends McpToolDeps { strategy: ...; allowedEmails: ...; oauth: ...; oauthAudit?: ...; rateLimitStore?: ...; }. The buildMcpServer call can then pass deps directly (structural subtyping satisfies McpToolDeps), eliminating the manual field-by-field reconstruction.
alternatives: Keep the parallel interfaces but add a compile-time test that asserts McpRoutesDeps is assignable to McpToolDeps.
Extract a shared McpCoreDeps base type that both extend without full inheritance.

findings.title: issueTokens creates access and refresh tokens in two non-atomic sequential inserts
findings.problem: The access token and refresh token are persisted in two separate await calls with no enclosing transaction. If the second insert fails (transient DB error), the access token row is already committed but the caller receives no tokens. The orphaned access token is verifiable and non-revoked but unused, accumulating over retries. There is also a brief window where the access token exists without a paired refresh token.
findings.impact: Under transient DB failure during token issuance, orphaned access tokens accumulate in the oauth_tokens table and the client must retry, potentially issuing multiple dangling rows.
findings.recommendation: Wrap both inserts in a single DB transaction so they commit or roll back atomically. If the OauthRepository's dual-dialect design makes a transaction difficult at the repo layer, parallelize the two inserts with Promise.all as a minimum improvement (no atomicity, but eliminates the sequential latency): await Promise.all([tokens.create(access...), tokens.create(refresh...)]).
alternatives: Use Promise.all to parallelize without a transaction, eliminating the latency cost while accepting the non-atomicity.
Document the known inconsistency and add a periodic cleanup for orphaned access tokens.

findings.title: rollback_canvas and deploy_canvas/begin_deploy missing NOT_ACTIVE status guard present on HTTP routes
findings.problem: The HTTP management deploy routes and the rollback route all check cv.status !== 'active' and return NOT_ACTIVE before proceeding. The MCP tools skip this check entirely. An agent can deploy new content or re-point the live version pointer on an archived or admin-disabled canvas. For disabled canvases, an admin takedown is supposed to freeze the live version; these tools allow an owner to change which historical version will be served (or what content is deployed) without the admin's knowledge, effective the moment the admin re-enables the canvas. The CLAUDE.md agent-native parity rule explicitly requires MCP tools to honour the same preconditions as the dashboard UI.
findings.impact: On a disabled canvas, an owner can silently pre-position new content via MCP that becomes live as soon as an admin re-enables the canvas, circumventing the intent of the admin action.
findings.recommendation: Add if (cv.status !== 'active') return fail('NOT_ACTIVE: unarchive this canvas before deploying or changing its live version') immediately after the requireOwned null guard in deploy_canvas, begin_deploy, and rollback_canvas, mirroring the pattern in publish_draft (draft-tools.ts line 180) and the management HTTP routes.
alternatives: Document this as an intentional design choice (key-auth Deploy API also lacks the guard) and note the asymmetry in a code comment.
Add the guard only to rollback_canvas (the most consequential) as a partial fix.

findings.title: set_canvas_preview silently discards encodeRenditions errors with no logging
findings.problem: The catch block for encodeRenditions(bytes) uses a bare catch { with no error binding and returns a generic INVALID_IMAGE message with no server-side logging. Failures here could include OOM conditions, codec crashes, or unexpected input format errors that would be invisible to operators. A caller who sends a valid-looking but unusually encoded image receives the same message as one who sends garbage.
findings.impact: Codec bugs, OOM during sharp decode, and unexpected image format errors are invisible to operators — no log entry, no audit record, no distinguishing signal.
findings.recommendation: Bind the error variable and log at warn level before returning the user-facing fail: catch (err) { log.warn({ err, canvasId: cv.id }, 'encodeRenditions failed'); return fail('INVALID_IMAGE: could not read that image'); }.
alternatives: Use deps.audit.recordAudit for the failure if the audit sink is the preferred observability channel.
Add only a console.warn as a minimal-effort fix without introducing a structured logger.

findings.title: set_canvas_preview has no byte-size cap before sharp decode, breaking parity with HTTP route
findings.problem: The HTTP PUT /:id/preview route is guarded by blobBodyLimit (maxSize: ~26 MB) before the body is buffered. The MCP set_canvas_preview tool accepts an arbitrary base64 string with no .max() on the Zod schema and no byte-count check after decoding. sharp's limitInputPixels bounds decoded pixel count (40 MP) but not the compressed byte count of the input. A caller can supply a large valid encoded image (e.g. an uncompressed BMP) that forces a large intermediate buffer allocation before sharp processes it. Combined with the absence of a body size limit on the /mcp endpoint itself, this is a targeted memory-pressure path.
findings.impact: An authenticated org member can submit a multi-hundred-megabyte base64 payload to set_canvas_preview, causing significant memory allocation on the server process with no early rejection.
findings.recommendation: Add a byte-length check after decoding: if (bytes.byteLength > LIMITS.maxFileBytes) return fail('IMAGE_TOO_LARGE: image exceeds the per-file limit'); importing LIMITS from the deploy errors module. Also add .max(40 * 1024 * 1024) to the Zod image string schema to reject oversized inputs before Buffer.from is called.
alternatives: Add only the post-decode byte check without the Zod schema .max(), accepting the base64 string will be fully allocated before rejection.
Address this finding together with the /mcp body-size limit finding (server-mcp-6) as a single defense-in-depth pass.

findings.title: /mcp endpoint has no HTTP body size limit before the JSON-RPC body is buffered
findings.problem: The /mcp route has no bodyLimit middleware before the transport reads the JSON-RPC body. deploy_canvas.zipBase64, deploy_canvas.files/add_files.files, and set_canvas_preview.image are all unbounded string or array parameters in the JSON-RPC body. The equivalent HTTP routes use blobBodyLimit and deployBodyLimit middlewares (deploy-common.ts lines 15-29) that reject oversized bodies before buffering. An authenticated org member can send a multi-GB JSON body to trigger OOM on a small VPS; the per-tool service-layer checks only fire after the body is already in memory.
findings.impact: An authenticated org member can send an arbitrarily large JSON-RPC payload to /mcp with no early rejection, causing memory exhaustion on a small VPS deployment.
findings.recommendation: Add a Hono bodyLimit middleware on the /mcp route, mounted before the bearer-auth check, sized to the largest legitimate payload (e.g. the full canvas cap: 100 MB + 10 MB headroom). The upstream HTTP routes set this precedent in deploy-common.ts.
alternatives: Apply per-tool Zod .max() constraints on the large string parameters as a cheaper partial mitigation without touching the transport layer.
Defer until the HTTP transport layer (hono/mcp) supports per-route size limits natively.

findings.title: set_canvas_preview has zero test coverage, including no cross-user ownership rejection test
findings.location: entire file — no test references set_canvas_preview
findings.problem: The set_canvas_preview tool handles two distinct code paths (clearing a custom preview, and uploading a new base64 image) with several branches in each path (previewMode guard on clear, byte-length check, encodeRenditions failure, storage write, mode transition). None are exercised. Additionally, set_canvas_preview and rollback_canvas are both absent from the exhaustive cross-user ownership-rejection (AE1) test loop, meaning a future refactor that inadvertently skips requireOwned for either tool would not be caught by the test suite.
findings.impact: Any regression in the decode-and-encode pipeline, the previewMode transition logic, the storage write, or the ownership check for set_canvas_preview would pass all tests undetected.
findings.recommendation: Add tests covering: (1) uploading a valid base64 image succeeds and transitions previewMode to 'custom'; (2) clearing when previewMode=custom deletes renditions and reverts to auto; (3) clearing when already in auto mode is a no-op; (4) an empty or invalid base64 image returns an isError response. Add set_canvas_preview and rollback_canvas to the AE1 tool-name loop in the cross-user rejection test.
alternatives: Add only the ownership-rejection tests as a minimal safety net, deferring the functional coverage to a later pass.
Add a regression test for encodeRenditions failure only, since the storage write is an integration-level concern.

findings.title: revalidateCanvas: DB errors on isPrincipalAllowed and isUserActive are fail-closed but silent
findings.problem: Both catch blocks correctly set `isAllowed = false` / `isActive = false` (fail-closed) but log nothing. A transient DB error during the periodic heartbeat sweep silently drops every user of a `specific_people` canvas (CLOSE 4401) or every connected user (isUserActive) with no server-side trace. An operator debugging spurious disconnections has no log entry to search for.
findings.impact: Spurious disconnections during DB hiccups are completely invisible. Operators cannot distinguish DB-error-induced drops from legitimate access revocations, making incident diagnosis very hard.
findings.recommendation: Log the caught error before failing closed in both catch blocks: `deps.log?.error({ err, canvasId, userId }, 'realtime: isPrincipalAllowed error — failing closed')`. Pass a logger via `HubDeps`.
alternatives: Emit a counter metric instead of a log line (observable via /metrics without log verbosity).
Accept silent fail-closed as-is; add an explicit integration test that injects DB errors and asserts disconnection.

findings.title: byCanvas map never prunes empty Sets — unbounded process-lifetime memory growth
findings.problem: `byCanvas: Map<string, Set<Conn>>` accumulates one empty `Set` entry per distinct canvas that has ever received a WebSocket connection. `dropConn` and `disconnect` delete the `Conn` from the Set but never call `byCanvas.delete(canvasId)` when the Set empties. `activeCanvasIds()` already filters `s.size > 0`, confirming awareness of the empty-Set state, but the cleanup write is missing. Over a long-running process with many canvases, the map grows proportional to all canvases ever connected, not just currently-active ones.
findings.impact: Slow, unbounded heap growth in a long-running server. The accumulation rate is proportional to unique canvases that receive any realtime traffic; on a busy instance with many canvases this is a meaningful leak.
findings.recommendation: After deleting a `Conn` in both `dropConn` and `disconnect`, check whether the Set is now empty and call `byCanvas.delete(conn.canvasId)`. One-liner addition in each of the two teardown paths.
alternatives: Add a periodic cleanup sweep over byCanvas to prune empty Sets (less precise but avoids touching the hot path).
Accept the leak if instance lifetimes are short (containers restarted on each deploy).

findings.title: AI /chat endpoint: no request body size limit — unbounded payload buffered into memory
findings.problem: `c.req.json()` is called with no preceding `bodyLimit` middleware. `chatSchema` validates structure but imposes no length cap on `messages[].content`, `system`, or the messages array size. All other large-body routes in the codebase use `bodyLimit` from `hono/body-limit` (e.g. `deployBodyLimit`, `blobBodyLimit`). A rate-limited session (aiPerMin=10) can still submit individual requests with multi-megabyte payloads, buffering the full body before Zod runs. The `AI_MAX_TOKENS` output cap does not constrain input token volume.
findings.impact: A member (trusted-org model) can send repeated large-body requests that spike memory and stall the event loop during JSON parsing. Under the rate limit a single session can keep ~10 MB/min flowing into the heap.
findings.recommendation: Add a `bodyLimit` middleware before the `/chat` handler (e.g. 256 KB). Add `.max(N)` constraints on `messages` array length and on `content`/`system` string fields in `chatSchema`.
alternatives: Set `AI_MAX_TOKENS` input-token equivalent as a heuristic body cap (no schema change needed if body limit alone is added).
Defer to a future hardening milestone given the trusted-org context, but document the gap.

findings.title: No per-connection channel subscription limit — unbounded conn.channels growth with broadcast amplification
findings.problem: A single WebSocket connection can subscribe to an unlimited number of distinct channel names. `doSubscribe` adds each unique channel string to `conn.channels` with no cap. The per-canvas connection limit (30) bounds connections but not per-connection channel count. Each novel subscribe triggers a `join` broadcast to all existing channel subscribers, so rapid novel-channel subscribes amplify broadcast load. `subscribers()` scans all connections for a canvas linearly — a connection subscribed to N channels makes every broadcast O(N * conns_per_canvas). Channel name length is also unbounded up to the 16 KB frame limit.
findings.impact: A member can exhaust server memory with large `conn.channels` Sets across 30 connections. Rapid subscriptions generate amplified broadcast fan-out that stalls other canvas connections during the linear scan.
findings.recommendation: Add `MAX_CHANNELS_PER_CONN` (e.g. 64) enforced at the top of `doSubscribe` with a `CHANNEL_LIMIT` error response. Add a maximum channel-name byte length check (e.g. 128 bytes) in `handleMessage` alongside the existing `MAX_MESSAGE_BYTES` guard.
alternatives: Count subscribe/unsubscribe frames against the existing rate-limit window to throttle subscription churn.
Accept the gap given the trusted-org threat model and document it as a known limit.

findings.title: Guest AI gate paths (GUEST_AI_DISABLED / GUEST_AI_CAP) entirely untested
findings.problem: The two guest-only rejection branches — `GUEST_AI_DISABLED` (403 when `canvas.guestAiEnabled` is false) and `GUEST_AI_CAP` (429 when canvas monthly spend exceeds `canvas.guestAiCap`) — are untested. The test suite never sets `c.get('principal')?.kind === 'guest'`, so both `if (isGuest && ...)` guards are permanently skipped. A regression allowing guests to consume unlimited AI, or misrouting the cap status code, would pass the full suite.
findings.impact: Guest AI cost controls can be silently regressed. An operator who sets `guestAiEnabled: false` on a canvas to lock it down would see that control bypassed with no test catching the failure.
findings.recommendation: Add two test cases: one that sets a guest principal with `guestAiEnabled=false` on the canvas and asserts 403 `GUEST_AI_DISABLED`; one that seeds canvas spend at or above `guestAiCap` and asserts 429 `GUEST_AI_CAP`.
alternatives: Add a single integration test covering both paths if unit-test isolation is complex.
Defer to a dedicated guest-AI test suite at the next milestone.

findings.title: Hub INVALID_FRAME and UNKNOWN_FRAME error paths not tested
findings.problem: The two wire-protocol error responses in `handleMessage` — `INVALID_FRAME` (non-JSON input) and `UNKNOWN_FRAME` (unrecognized frame type) — are not exercised by any test. The suite covers `MESSAGE_TOO_LARGE` and `RATE_LIMITED` but not parse errors. A regression silently swallowing JSON parse errors (e.g. removing the try/catch) would stop emitting `INVALID_FRAME` with no test catching it.
findings.impact: Wire-protocol error paths are unverifiable; regressions go undetected until clients report mysterious behavior.
findings.recommendation: Add two tests: one calling `hub.handleMessage(conn, 'not-json')` asserting `{ type: 'error', code: 'INVALID_FRAME' }`, one sending `JSON.stringify({ type: 'bogus' })` asserting `{ code: 'UNKNOWN_FRAME' }`.
alternatives: Cover both cases in a single parameterized test.

findings.title: renderGuestInvite returns to: '' — type implies valid recipient, footgun for future callers
findings.problem: The return type is `EmailMessage & { to: string }`, implying `to` is a populated field. The actual value is always `""` with a comment 'filled by the caller.' A future caller that forgets to override `to` sends email to an empty address with no type error. The current single caller (guest-invite.ts) correctly spreads `{ ...msg, to: email }`, but the type system offers no protection.
findings.impact: A future caller omitting `to` silently sends email with an empty recipient. The error would be caught only at the SMTP/Mailgun layer, not at development time.
findings.recommendation: Remove `to` from the return type: return `Omit<EmailMessage, 'to'>`. The caller's spread `{ ...msg, to: email }` still works as-is, but forgetting `to` becomes a compile error.
alternatives: Accept the recipient email as a parameter to `renderGuestInvite` and return a complete `EmailMessage` with a populated `to`.
Add a JSDoc `@throws` note and an assertion `if (!to) throw` in the mailer's `send()` method.

findings.title: DraftPreview fullscreen overlay has no dialog role, focus trap, or Escape handler
findings.problem: The fullscreen overlay is a `<div className='fixed inset-0 z-50 ...'>` with no `role='dialog'`, no `aria-modal='true'`, no focus trap, and no Escape key handler. Screen reader users in virtual cursor mode can navigate to background content while the overlay is open. Keyboard users who Tab through the overlay will exit into the background document.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users cannot reliably stay inside the fullscreen preview or exit it via Escape; they must Tab through the entire overlay and then continue into the hidden background.
findings.recommendation: Apply the same focus-trap pattern used by Dialog.tsx and DetailDrawer.tsx: add `role='dialog'`, `aria-modal='true'`, `aria-label='Full screen preview'`, `tabIndex={-1}` on the overlay container, and use a `useEffect` to save the previously focused element, move focus into the overlay on open, and restore on close. Add an Escape key listener that calls `onToggleFullscreen`.
alternatives: Use the existing Dialog component instead of a raw div, which would get the focus trap and Escape handling for free.
Add only the Escape key handler as a minimal fix, acknowledging the focus-trap gap as a deferred improvement.

findings.title: AccessLadder fieldset uses aria-label without a <legend> — less reliably announced across AT/browser pairs
findings.problem: The access rung radio group uses `<fieldset aria-label='Who can access this canvas'>` without a `<legend>`. While `aria-label` on a fieldset is valid per spec, `<legend>` is the semantically correct and most reliably announced way to label a radio group across all AT/browser combinations. Some older screen reader versions read `aria-label` on a fieldset inconsistently.
findings.impact: Screen reader users may not hear the group label 'Who can access this canvas' before navigating the access-rung radio buttons on the Share tab.
findings.recommendation: Add a visually-hidden `<legend className='sr-only'>Who can access this canvas</legend>` inside the fieldset and remove the `aria-label` attribute.
alternatives: Keep the aria-label and accept the minor AT inconsistency given canvas-drop targets modern browsers.
Move the group label above the fieldset as a visible heading instead of a legend.

findings.title: api.ts God Module: wire types and HTTP client are fused in one 972-line file with 38+ consumers
findings.problem: api.ts serves two completely different roles: the canonical wire-type registry (~30 exported types, ~680 lines) and the runtime HTTP client (`request`, `xhrUpload`, `api` object, `ApiError`, `onAuthExpired`). Any type change (even a comment) touches the same module as HTTP mechanics. With 38 source-file consumers, every lint or typecheck failure in this file blocks the entire dashboard from compiling.
findings.impact: Merge conflicts between type changes and HTTP client changes are frequent. Tree-shaking is impossible across the combined type/client surface. Test stubs that only need types must import the full HTTP machinery.
findings.recommendation: Split into `lib/types.ts` (all interfaces, type aliases, constants) and `lib/client.ts` (request, xhrUpload, ApiError, onAuthExpired, api). Barrel-export both from a thin `lib/api.ts` to avoid breaking existing imports in a single migration pass.
alternatives: Extract only the most stable types (Canvas, Me, DraftView) into lib/types.ts as a first step, deferring the full split.
Accept the current structure given the dashboard is a single-app with no package boundary to enforce separation.

findings.title: Allowlist component uses raw api.* calls and local useState instead of React Query — inconsistent with every other fetch surface
findings.problem: The `Allowlist` component is the only data-fetching surface in the dashboard that bypasses React Query. It uses raw `useState<AllowlistEntry[] | null>` with a manual `reload()` closure, resulting in: no cache (data is lost on navigation and refetched every time), no cancellation on unmount (setEntries can fire on an unmounted instance), two sequential round-trips per mutation (mutate + reload), and error handling that discards the ApiError rather than surfacing it. All other data surfaces (canvases, versions, draft, usage, admin tables, admin config) use useQuery/useMutation.
findings.impact: On every access to the Share tab for a canvas with allowlist members, two sequential network requests fire. Cache invalidation from a settings mutation cannot coherently update the allowlist. The pattern is an inconsistency tax for every contributor reading the fetch layer.
findings.recommendation: Add `useAllowlist(canvasId)` to queries.ts (using `useQuery` keyed as `['allowlist', canvasId]`) and migrate add/remove/resend to mutations.ts (using `useMutation` with `onSuccess: () => qc.invalidateQueries(keys.allowlist(canvasId))`). The component then matches every other data surface in the dashboard.
alternatives: Keep local state but add a `mountedRef` guard in reload's `.then` callback and add a busy guard to `remove()` to prevent concurrent removals — a narrower fix without the full migration.
Defer to when the allowlist gains additional consumers (e.g. a future API-level or admin surface) that would benefit from shared cache.

findings.title: Dialog body-scroll lock can permanently disable page scrolling when two dialogs overlap during exit transitions
findings.problem: The Dialog open-effect captures `prevOverflow = document.body.style.overflow` before setting it to `'hidden'`. If a second dialog opens while the first is in its 150ms exit transition (still mounted with `open=false`), the second captures `prevOverflow = 'hidden'`. When the first's cleanup runs it restores `overflow = ''` (removing the lock while the second is open). When the second closes, it restores `overflow = 'hidden'`, permanently locking the page.
findings.impact: A user who triggers two dialogs in quick succession (e.g. a ConfirmDialog opening while CloneDialog transitions out) may end up with the page permanently unscrollable after both dialogs close. Requires a hard page refresh to fix.
findings.recommendation: Use a ref-counted approach: a module-level counter increments when any dialog opens (setting overflow='hidden' on the first) and decrements when it closes (restoring on the last reaching zero). Alternatively, apply the lock via a CSS class on `<body>` rather than an inline style, so the final removal is always deterministic.
alternatives: Gate the scroll lock to only the top-most Dialog in a portal stack, which would require Dialog to detect whether another Dialog is already open.
Accept the risk given the dashboard generally avoids nested dialogs and the exit-transition window is narrow.

findings.title: useSlugAvailability network errors silently return to idle with no user or diagnostic signal
findings.problem: When the `slugAvailable` API call fails, the catch block sets status back to `'idle'` with no logging and no user notification. The server re-validates on submit so this does not allow a taken slug through, but a persistent network issue leaves the availability check permanently bouncing between 'checking' and 'idle' with no user escape and no diagnostic information.
findings.impact: A user creating a canvas during a transient network issue sees no availability indicator and no error — they cannot tell whether the slug check is running, failed, or simply not applicable. Support diagnosis is also hindered.
findings.recommendation: At minimum add `console.warn('canvas-drop: slug availability check failed', err)` in the catch block. Optionally set a distinct `'error'` status and show a brief inline notice ('Could not check availability — the server will validate on submit').
alternatives: Migrate to `useQuery` with `enabled: !!slug` to get React Query's built-in error state and retry logic.
Accept current behavior as sufficient given server-side revalidation on submit.

findings.title: deployCurl is called twice with identical arguments in canvas.settings.tsx and has no unit tests
findings.problem: `deployCurl(...)` is called twice in the same render with identical arguments — once for `<CopyButton value={...}>` and once for the `<pre>` text content. The function has no unit tests; if its path template or header name changes, the displayed command silently becomes incorrect and users' deploys fail.
findings.impact: Minor: two identical function calls per render (pure function, negligible cost). No tests means a future refactor of the curl format has no regression safety net.
findings.recommendation: Compute the snippet once: `const curlSnippet = deployCurl({ url: canvas.url, id: canvas.id, apiKey: '$CANVAS_DROP_KEY' })` and reuse it. Add 3–4 unit tests covering folder mode, zip mode, API key substitution, and the URL path format.
alternatives: Memoize with useMemo if the render is hot (it is not — this is a settings page).
Accept the duplicate call as inconsequential and focus test effort on higher-risk helpers.

findings.title: Paginated result-label computation (from/to/hasPrev/hasNext) is duplicated verbatim across four routes
findings.problem: All four paginated routes independently compute identical `from`, `to`, `hasPrev`, `hasNext` values from `total`, `offset`, `items.length`, and `page` using the same `Math.min(offset + items.length, total)` clamp. Any off-by-one fix or naming change must be applied in four files.
findings.impact: Low: the computation is simple and the four copies have remained in sync. But it is unnecessary duplication that will diverge under refactoring pressure.
findings.recommendation: Extract `usePagination({ total, offset, items, pageSize })` returning `{ from, to, hasPrev, hasNext, page }` and use it in all four routes.
alternatives: Accept the duplication given the simplicity of the computation and the low risk of divergence.
Consolidate just the from/to clamp into a shared utility function without the full hook.

findings.title: Canvas.status typed as bare string while PublicationState is a proper union — two parallel lifecycle representations with no exhaustiveness checking
findings.problem: The `Canvas` interface carries both `status: string` (raw DB column) and `publicationState: PublicationState` (derived union). The dashboard branches on `canvas.status === 'active'` in at least six locations while also using `canvas.publicationState` for badge rendering. Because `status` is `string`, TypeScript cannot flag a typo or a missed exhaustive arm. A future status value added to the server is silently accepted as an unknown string.
findings.impact: A typo in a status comparison (e.g. `'actve'`) compiles without error and silently falls through all branches. A new server status value added without updating the dashboard produces no compiler warning.
findings.recommendation: Define `type CanvasStatus = 'active' | 'archived' | 'disabled' | 'deleted'` and change `status: string` to `status: CanvasStatus`. Consider whether `publicationState` should fully replace `status` for all UI branching, leaving `status` only for raw admin display.
alternatives: Add a branded `CanvasStatus` type in shared/wire-types so both server and dashboard consume the same definition.
Accept the current design since `publicationState` covers all UI branching and `status` is only used in a small number of explicit comparisons.

findings.title: AdminConfigField uses a flat optional type where two mutually exclusive shapes (secret vs non-secret) should be a discriminated union
findings.problem: `AdminConfigField` mixes `value?: string` (for non-secret fields) and `set?: boolean; last4?: string` (for secret fields) in a flat optional interface, making combinations like `{ secret: false, set: true, last4: 'abcd' }` representable. Callers must know to check `field.secret` first; TypeScript provides no help.
findings.impact: A caller that reads `field.set` without checking `field.secret` first compiles cleanly but may produce incorrect UI (e.g. showing a 'secret set' indicator on a plain-text config field). Future callers adding admin config types face no guidance.
findings.recommendation: Replace with a discriminated union: `type AdminConfigField = Base & ({ secret: false; value: string | undefined } | { secret: true; set: boolean; last4?: string })`. Callers that narrow on `secret` then get proper type inference for the sub-fields.
alternatives: Keep the flat type but add JSDoc documenting the mutual exclusivity, which at least guides future callers even without compiler enforcement.
Extract a small helper `isSecretField(f): f is SecretConfigField` that callers use as a type guard.

findings.title: RootEntry path/reason pairing is not encoded in the type — consumers can read path without narrowing on reason
findings.problem: `RootEntry` is `{ path: string | null; reason: 'index'|'single'|'ambiguous'|'none' }`. The comment documents that `path` is null when reason is 'ambiguous' or 'none', but the type does not enforce this. canvas.overview.tsx line 234 reads `current?.entry.path` without narrowing on reason — TypeScript requires a null-check but cannot verify the caller understands which reasons imply non-null.
findings.impact: A consumer that checks `reason === 'index'` and then uses `path` without a null assertion will get a TypeScript error and add `!` — which is correct for that reason but trains the codebase to use `!` assertions on a field that is genuinely null for other reasons.
findings.recommendation: Model as a discriminated union: `type RootEntry = { path: string; reason: 'index'|'single' } | { path: null; reason: 'ambiguous'|'none' }`. TypeScript then narrows `path` to `string` after `reason === 'index'`.
alternatives: Add a type guard `hasRoot(e: RootEntry): e is { path: string; reason: 'index'|'single' }` instead of restructuring the interface.
Accept the current design since callers already perform null-checks and the comment documents the invariant.

findings.title: Spurious @opentelemetry/api production dependency in packages/shared — never imported
findings.problem: @opentelemetry/api is listed as a production dependency of packages/shared but zero source files under packages/shared/src import from it. It is dead weight that inflates node_modules and could cause unexpected peer-dep resolution conflicts if the SDK ever bundles shared for a browser target.
findings.impact: Build tool confusion if the shared package is ever bundled for the browser (esbuild attempts to bundle a Node-specific package). Misleads contributors into thinking the shared package exports telemetry hooks.
findings.recommendation: Remove @opentelemetry/api from packages/shared/package.json dependencies. If OpenTelemetry instrumentation is added to the server in the future, add it to apps/server/package.json where it will be used.
alternatives: Move it to devDependencies if it is only used for type definitions in a planned (not yet implemented) telemetry layer.
Defer removal until a bundling concern is actually observed.

findings.title: usage_events schema comment labels 'view' and 'rt_connect' as future — both are already live
findings.problem: The comment reads 'future: view | deploy | rt_connect' but all five types are actively used: recordView writes 'view' rows and canvas-realtime.ts writes 'rt_connect' rows. The stale 'future:' qualifier is misleading to anyone reading the schema.
findings.impact: A developer reading this comment may incorrectly assume view and rt_connect events are unimplemented and skip handling them in new code that reads usage_events, or omit them from a retention/prune job.
findings.recommendation: Update the comment in both schema files to list all live types without the 'future:' qualifier: 'Current types: kv_op | file_op | view | deploy | rt_connect'.
alternatives: Also export a UsageEventType = 'kv_op' | 'file_op' | 'view' | 'deploy' | 'rt_connect' union type from types.ts (consistent with the missing-union-types pattern flagged elsewhere).

findings.title: SDK module-level JSDoc omits ai and realtime primitives from the enumerated API surface
findings.problem: The module comment enumerates 'canvasdrop.kv, canvasdrop.files, and canvasdrop.me()' but omits canvasdrop.ai and canvasdrop.realtime, both of which are fully implemented in the same file. A developer reading only the header gets an incomplete picture of the SDK's API surface.
findings.impact: Developers discovering the SDK via documentation or IDE hover will not see ai and realtime in the module description, potentially causing them to miss those primitives or look for them elsewhere.
findings.recommendation: Expand the enumeration: 'Canvas code calls canvasdrop.kv, canvasdrop.files, canvasdrop.ai, canvasdrop.realtime, and canvasdrop.me(); the SDK derives the canvas slug + API base from location, sends credentialed requests, and throws typed errors.'
alternatives: Leave as-is and rely on the API reference documentation — low priority if generated docs are the primary discovery path.

findings.title: brand.ts references a non-existent REBRAND.md file
findings.problem: The comment says 'see REBRAND.md' but no REBRAND.md exists anywhere in the repository. A self-hoster following this pointer for rebranding instructions finds nothing.
findings.impact: Self-hosters and contributors attempting to rebrand the product following the in-code guidance will hit a dead link, degrading the self-hosting experience which is a core product goal.
findings.recommendation: Either create REBRAND.md with rebranding instructions, or remove the 'see REBRAND.md' clause and fold the guidance inline (e.g., 'edit brand.ts for name/domain/fonts and tokens.ts for colors/logo').
alternatives: Point to an existing docs page (e.g., a rebranding section in the README or BUILD_BRIEF) if that guidance already exists elsewhere.

findings.title: rate-limit comment implies admin-tunable limits are available — they are explicitly deferred
findings.problem: The comment reads 'admin-tunable rate limits are a follow-up' in a way that implies the feature is planned but the current values are admin-visible. In reality, the admin config UI marks all seven rate-limit fields editable: false with the explicit note 'Rate limits are read-only for now'. The comment overstates current functionality.
findings.impact: A developer looking for where to hook in runtime rate-limit changes may waste time searching the admin UI for a toggle that does not exist.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite: 'Per-class req/min enforcement constants; read at boot from env only (not yet runtime-editable via the admin UI — deliberate follow-up).'
alternatives: Leave as-is given the comment is accurate in intent if not in current state — lowest priority fix.

findings.title: galleryTemplatable → galleryListed invariant enforced only in code, not by a DB CHECK constraint
findings.location: lines 152-156 (canvases table, galleryTemplatable column); also schema.sqlite.ts
findings.problem: The column comment documents the invariant 'only ever true when galleryListed is true — every path that clears galleryListed also clears this in the same write.' There is no DB CHECK constraint enforcing this. A future service-layer path that sets galleryTemplatable=true without also setting galleryListed=true (or clears galleryListed without clearing galleryTemplatable) would produce an inconsistent row silently accepted by the schema.
findings.impact: A canvas could appear as templatable in the gallery without appearing as gallery-listed, or remain marked templatable after being de-listed, potentially showing stale template options to users.
findings.recommendation: Add `check('canvases_gallery_templatable_chk', sql\`${t.galleryTemplatable} = false OR ${t.galleryListed} = true\`)` in both schema files. This is a constant-time CHECK with no index cost.
alternatives: Add a service-layer assertion that throws if the invariant is violated before any write, deferring the schema change.
Add a schema.test.ts test that asserts the constraint exists in both dialects, which at least documents the invariant even if not enforced at the DB layer yet.

findings.title: Missing tests for kv.list, kv.delete, files.list, and files.delete
findings.problem: kv.get, kv.set, kv.increment, and kv.user.set are covered. kv.list (which conditionally appends ?prefix=&cursor=&limit= query parameters), kv.delete, files.list, and files.delete have no test at all. URL construction logic with conditional query parameters is easy to silently break.
findings.impact: A regression in kv.list URL building (wrong parameter name, dropped option) would cause unexpected 400 errors or wrong paginated results at runtime, with no CI gate catching it.
findings.recommendation: Add tests for: (1) kv.list with all options verifying the full query string, (2) kv.list with no options verifying a plain URL, (3) kv.delete verifying DELETE method and URL, (4) files.list verifying GET /files and response parsing, (5) files.delete verifying DELETE /files/{id}.
alternatives: Add at minimum a kv.list test covering the query-string construction path — that is the highest-regression-risk gap given the conditional parameter logic.

findings.title: Several constrained text columns lack exported TypeScript union types — string comparisons are unchecked
findings.location: lines 31-109 (types.ts); affected columns across schema files
findings.problem: Multiple columns carry string-domain constraints enforced only at the DB CHECK layer without a corresponding TypeScript union type in types.ts. Affected columns: mcp_tokens.kind ('access' | 'refresh'), guest_invites.state ('pending' | 'active' | 'revoked'), versions.source ('folder' | 'zip' | 'paste' | 'api' | 'editor' | 'upload'), versions.status ('pending' | 'ready'), usage_events.type ('kv_op' | 'file_op' | 'view' | 'deploy' | 'rt_connect'). Without named union types, callers use raw string from the inferred row types, and a typo like token.kind === 'Access' is a silent bug.
findings.impact: Type comparisons on these columns are unchecked at compile time. A typo or stale string literal in a service-layer condition silently evaluates false (e.g., `token.kind === 'Access'` never matches 'access'), causing auth/state logic to silently misbehave.
findings.recommendation: Export union types for each domain alongside the existing CanvasStatus, ScreenshotJobStatus, etc.: McpTokenKind, GuestInviteState, VersionSource, VersionStatus, UsageEventType. Use .$type<McpTokenKind>() on the column definition to thread them into the schema inference.
alternatives: Use z.enum() in the Zod parse layer at service boundaries as a runtime guard without changing the schema types (partial fix — doesn't help type inference at the repository level).
Defer and address only the most risky one (e.g., McpTokenKind for token auth logic) rather than all five at once.

findings.title: admin/config-fields.ts imports from routes/canvas-kv.ts forming an inverted cross-layer dependency
findings.problem: The admin config registry (config-fields.ts) imports default quota constants from a route handler module (routes/canvas-kv.ts). routes/canvas-kv.ts in turn imports QuotaResolver (type-only) from admin/settings-service.ts, which imports config-fields.ts, forming a triangular dependency. The architectural layering is inverted: an admin config registry depends on a route handler module to obtain numeric constants.
findings.impact: Any refactoring of canvas-kv.ts constants must account for the admin registry's dependency. As the editable field set grows, this inverted dependency makes the module boundaries harder to maintain.
findings.recommendation: Extract KV_MAX_KEYS_SHARED, KV_MAX_KEYS_USER, and analogous file-size constants into a shared limits module (e.g. apps/server/src/primitives/limits.ts or packages/shared). Both route handlers and config-fields.ts import from there, eliminating the downward dependency.
alternatives: Inline the default values directly in config-fields.ts as local constants, removing the import entirely (appropriate if the constants are never used by other callers outside config-fields).
Accept the current coupling given the small surface area and defer until a second consumer makes the shared module worthwhile.

findings.title: Stale comment in settings-service.ts incorrectly states the boolean branch is unreachable
findings.problem: The comment reads 'No editable field is boolean or enum today... so these branches are unreached until the editable set grows; kept so that's a one-line flip.' This is factually incorrect: screenshots.enabled is declared editable:true and type:'boolean' in config-fields.ts, and settings-service.test.ts explicitly tests the boolean branch. A developer reading this comment would incorrectly believe the boolean case is dead scaffolding.
findings.impact: Misleads future contributors into treating an active, tested code path as unreachable, potentially causing it to be removed or skipped during refactoring.
findings.recommendation: Update the comment to: 'boolean: used by screenshots.enabled; enum: not yet editable (flip settingKey when needed).'
alternatives: Remove the comment entirely since the code is self-explanatory.
Add a test assertion comment pointing to the specific test that covers the boolean path.

findings.title: OIDC no_email_claim and email_not_verified error paths return raw JSON mid-browser-flow
findings.problem: Four post-exchange error paths return c.json({error: ...}) directly instead of going through recoverableAuthError, which renders an HTML page with a 'Try signing in again' action. The no_email_claim path (line 189) also has no log line. These are rare but visible paths: a user mid-login flow in a browser sees a raw JSON payload with no recovery action and no next step. The recoverableAuthError helper exists precisely for this and is used for the other mid-flow errors (token_exchange_failed, state_mismatch).
findings.impact: Users whose IdP emits no email claim, marks email_verified=false, or hits a blocked/not-allowed case receive a broken browser experience — a raw JSON payload with no retry button — requiring manual navigation to restart login.
findings.recommendation: Route no_email_claim, email_not_verified, email_domain_not_allowed, and (for non-security-sensitive cases) forbidden through recoverableAuthError with appropriate title and message values. Add a log.warn for no_email_claim (currently has no log at all). The forbidden/blocked case can use a generic 'You don't have access to this application' message.
alternatives: Accept raw JSON for the forbidden/blocked case for intentional opacity, but apply recoverableAuthError to the other three paths only.
Add a client-side JS redirect on the error page to handle these in the SPA rather than the server.

findings.title: Email addresses and IP addresses stored indefinitely in audit_log with no retention mechanism
findings.problem: Email addresses flow into audit_log.meta for auth_denied (domain_not_allowed reason), guest_login, and guest_invite events. IP addresses flow into audit_log.ip for all auth events. Both are stored with no TTL, expiry column, or automated deletion. The AuditRepository only exposes append() and recent() — no prune/delete path. Right-to-erasure requests cannot be honored without bespoke tooling. This affects the sessions table too (sessions.ip stored after revocation; revokedAt is set but rows are never hard-deleted).
findings.impact: GDPR/CCPA right-to-erasure obligations cannot be fulfilled for users who request deletion of their data. IP addresses (PII under GDPR) and email addresses accumulate indefinitely in audit and session storage.
findings.recommendation: Replace raw email addresses in audit meta with pseudonymous identifiers (e.g. the email domain only for domain-rejection events; omit email entirely for guest_login/guest_invite where targetId already identifies the canvas). Add a retainUntil column to audit_log and a periodic purge job. For sessions, add a hard-delete sweep for rows where expiresAt < now() - grace_period.
alternatives: Add a repository-level pruneOlderThan(cutoff) method and call it from a scheduled job without changing the schema.
Defer until right-to-erasure requests arise in practice, but document the retention gap in operator docs.

findings.title: User-Agent stored in sessions table but never read — unnecessary PII collection
findings.problem: The HTTP User-Agent header is collected at session creation and persisted in sessions.userAgent. User-Agent strings are specific device fingerprints (OS version, browser build). However, userAgent is stored but never read by any repository or service in the codebase — it is pure data collection with no functional use. Revoked sessions are soft-deleted (revokedAt set, row retained), so User-Agent accumulates indefinitely after revocation.
findings.impact: Unnecessary collection of a device fingerprint that cannot be honored in right-to-erasure requests and creates no product value in its current unused state.
findings.recommendation: Stop collecting User-Agent: remove the userAgent field from the sessions.create call and the DB column. If device-aware anomaly detection is a future roadmap item, document the justification before re-adding. As a lighter alternative, add a hard-delete purge for expired/revoked session rows.
alternatives: Keep collecting but add a background purge of revoked/expired rows so the data does not accumulate indefinitely.
Defer until a right-to-erasure request is received, then decide whether collection is justified.

findings.title: claimsToIdentity has no direct unit tests — sub-fallback and edge cases untested
findings.location: identity-mapping.ts:13-23 — no claimsToIdentity tests in identity-mapping.test.ts
findings.problem: claimsToIdentity is used by both the proxy and OIDC strategies, making it a shared identity-extraction seam. Its sub-fallback logic (when claims.sub is absent or empty, falls back to email — line 19) and the provider namespace prefix are critical for avoiding provider_sub collisions. No direct tests exist for edge cases: missing sub, empty sub, missing name/picture, non-string types.
findings.impact: Edge-case regressions (e.g. a provider sending sub: '' or sub: 0) could cause identity collisions or unexpected null returns, silently breaking authentication for affected users.
findings.recommendation: Add a describe('claimsToIdentity') block in identity-mapping.test.ts covering: well-formed claims produce correct namespaced sub; absent/empty sub falls back to email; absent email returns null; optional name/avatarUrl omitted when missing. Pure in-memory logic, no DB dependency.
alternatives: Add coverage via the proxy/OIDC integration tests with well-targeted edge-case fixtures rather than a dedicated unit test block.

findings.title: makeOidcConfigLoader retry-on-failure behavior is untested
findings.problem: makeOidcConfigLoader exports the property 'a failed discovery must not be cached — the next call retries', which is the exact guard that prevents a transient network error at first boot from permanently disabling OIDC. The function is exported but has no test. If the .catch(() => { cached = undefined }) guard were accidentally dropped, a single transient error would permanently break OIDC for the process lifetime.
findings.impact: A regression removing the failure-clears-cache behavior would go undetected until a production transient network error at startup permanently breaks OIDC login for all users for the duration of the process.
findings.recommendation: Add a two-step test: (1) create a loader whose discovery promise rejects on the first call, assert the first call rejects; (2) swap to a succeeding discovery mock, assert the second call succeeds. This verifies the failure does not poison the loader.
alternatives: Accept that this is covered implicitly by integration tests if an IdP flakiness scenario is included.
Add a code comment only, without a test, acknowledging the invariant.

findings.title: ConfigField invariants (editable↔settingKey, secret↔value) are runtime-only, not structural types
findings.problem: Two related invariants are enforced only at runtime: (1) every editable ConfigField must have a settingKey — enforced by throws in setConfigOverride/clearConfigOverride; (2) ConfigFieldView uses optional value/set/last4 fields on a flat interface instead of a discriminated union, so secret rows could be type-safely read for value (undefined, not a compile error). New config fields added without settingKey when editable:true are caught only at test/runtime, not at compile time.
findings.impact: A developer adding a new editable config field without settingKey gets a runtime throw rather than a compile error. A future consumer of ConfigFieldView could accidentally access .value on a secret field without a type narrowing requirement.
findings.recommendation: For ConfigField: use a discriminated union on editable — type ConfigField = ConfigFieldBase & ({ editable: true; settingKey: string } | { editable: false; settingKey?: never }). For ConfigFieldView: use a discriminated union on secret — ({ secret: true; set: boolean; last4?: string } | { secret: false; value: string }).
alternatives: Add a Zod validator for the CONFIG_FIELDS array at module load time that throws on misconfiguration instead of changing the TypeScript types.
Accept the runtime throws as sufficient enforcement given the small number of config field contributors.

findings.title: deploy/engine and draft/service duplicate version-number retry logic; KEEP_VERSIONS cross-layer import
findings.location: lines 237-257, createVersionWithRetry(); apps/server/src/draft/service.ts line 11 (import) and lines 227-248
findings.problem: Both deploy/engine.ts and draft/service.ts implement the identical next-number-then-insert retry loop. Additionally, draft/service.ts imports KEEP_VERSIONS from deploy/engine.ts, creating a bidirectional coupling: the deploy layer imports from the draft repo and the draft layer imports from the deploy engine. KEEP_VERSIONS is a domain constant that belongs in a neutral location shared by both.
findings.impact: Changing the retry count or collision detection logic requires two edits in sync. A developer reading draft/service.ts must navigate to deploy/engine.ts to understand a constant. No runtime failure mode, but the coupling is a maintenance hazard.
findings.recommendation: Extract the collision-retrying version-number insert into a shared helper in the versions repository or a new deploy/constants.ts. Move KEEP_VERSIONS to deploy/constants.ts (or shared errors.ts alongside LIMITS) so both consumers import from a neutral location.
alternatives: Move KEEP_VERSIONS only (the smaller change) and leave the retry logic duplicated — the duplication is less dangerous than the circular import.
Defer until the retry logic needs to change, using the refactor as a forcing function.

findings.title: Stale comment in capability-guard.ts: 'No primitive routes exist yet'
findings.problem: The module docstring says 'No primitive routes exist yet — this module ships the guard and its tests so the primitives are a thin handler behind one shared check'. KV, Files, AI, and identity primitive routes all exist and use requireCapability (shipped in M6/M9). The phrase 'future backend primitives' also misdirects. A maintainer reading this module would incorrectly conclude the guard is unused in production.
findings.impact: A developer reading the module may mistakenly believe requireCapability is not yet in use and treat it as provisional infrastructure, leading to incorrect refactoring assumptions.
findings.recommendation: Remove 'No primitive routes exist yet' and 'future backend primitives'. Revised: 'Capability runtime guard (plan 006). The seam each backend primitive (KV/Files/AI/Realtime/identity) plugs into: each primitive route group runs requireCapability(cap, config) after canvasAccess has resolved + authorized the canvas, and gets a typed 403 when the capability is off.'
alternatives: Delete the entire historical framing paragraph and leave only the per-function JSDoc comments, which are already accurate.

findings.title: Inaccurate docstring: resolveAccessContext says only specific_people needs a DB lookup
findings.problem: The docstring states 'Only the specific_people rung needs the lookup; other rungs short-circuit to no context.' The implementation also performs a DB lookup for the public_link rung (isOwnerPublishEnabled). A developer relying on the comment to understand I/O cost would be misled.
findings.impact: Developers optimizing the request path may skip caching or batching the public_link lookup because the comment implies it is free. No runtime failure.
findings.recommendation: Update to: 'The public_link rung checks whether the owner's publish capability is enabled; the specific_people rung checks the allowlist. All other rungs short-circuit to empty context.'
alternatives: Add a one-line inline comment at the public_link branch instead of updating the top-level docstring.

findings.title: createVersionWithRetry and createReadyVersion retry all errors, not just unique-constraint collisions
findings.problem: Both retry loops catch every exception from createPending without discriminating between the intended case (unique-constraint collision on canvas_id+number) and unrelated errors (DB connection loss, permission errors, disk full). A connection timeout will be retried 4 unnecessary extra times, potentially compounding latency and masking the root cause. There is also no logging during the retry loop itself, so operators cannot distinguish 'retried and succeeded' from 'retried 5 times and then failed' in logs.
findings.impact: A DB connection error during deploy or publish causes up to 5× unnecessary retry latency before surfacing the failure. The lack of per-attempt logging makes the retry invisible to operators, who see only the final error without context.
findings.recommendation: Guard the retry on a constraint-violation predicate (check error message for 'UNIQUE' or use a dialect-aware helper). Log at debug/warn level on each retry attempt including the attempt number and error. Log at error level on retry exhaustion.
alternatives: Use a separate nextNumber + insert-with-conflict-target approach (e.g. INSERT OR IGNORE in SQLite) to eliminate the retry entirely.
Accept the current broad-catch retry as low-risk given the collision is the only realistic failure mode at current scale, and just add logging.

findings.title: uploadService: log dep declared in UploadServiceDeps but never used; clearFinalizing failure is fully silent
findings.location: line 43, UploadServiceDeps.log; line 299, finalize catch block
findings.problem: The log?: Logger field in UploadServiceDeps is declared but never referenced anywhere in the file. The production instantiation in app.ts omits it entirely. As a result, when clearFinalizing(claimed.id) fails inside the finalize catch block (.catch(() => {})), the failure is completely silent. If the upload session's in-progress lease cannot be released, the session stays in 'finalizing' state for 60s with no server-side trace.
findings.impact: Repeated clearFinalizing failures cause clients to receive UPLOAD_IN_PROGRESS errors for up to a minute with no operator-visible log. The dead log field gives false confidence that logging is wired.
findings.recommendation: Either use the log dep by wiring deps.log?.warn(...) inside the .catch, or remove the dead log?: Logger field. At minimum wire it into the clearFinalizing failure path.
alternatives: Remove the log field entirely and route the failure through the calling route's global error handler.
Add a TODO comment on the field noting it is intentionally unused pending a future log wiring.

findings.title: clone-service and verifyPassword rollback/error paths fully silent — no Logger dep
findings.problem: When blob copying or draft seeding fails mid-clone, the rollback attempts canvases.setStatus(canvas.id, 'deleted') and storage.deleteMany(...), both with .catch(() => {}) and no logging. CloneServiceDeps has no Logger field. If the soft-delete fails (e.g. DB unavailable), the canvas row remains 'active' with an incomplete draft — purge only processes status='deleted' canvases. Separately, verifyPassword catches all argon2 errors and returns false with no logging, making system-level failures in the auth primitive invisible (audit log records success: false, indistinguishable from a wrong password).
findings.impact: A failed mid-clone rollback can leave a half-cloned canvas active and visible to the owner. A system-level argon2 failure is audited as a wrong-password attempt, masking infrastructure problems.
findings.recommendation: Add an optional log?: Logger to CloneServiceDeps and log at warn inside each rollback .catch(). For verifyPassword, log unexpected errors at warn before returning false, distinguishing known malformed-hash errors from unexpected ones.
alternatives: For clone-service, use a transaction for the canvas row creation and draft seeding so a rollback is automatic on failure.
Accept both as low-risk at trusted-org scale and add log wiring in a future observability pass.

findings.title: Sequential storage.exists()+put() per blob doubles round-trips in deploy and draft write hot paths
findings.problem: Both putBlobIfAbsent (deploy engine) and draftService.writeFile call storage.exists(key) before storage.put(key, bytes), doubling storage round-trips. On S3/MinIO each call is a network RTT. A 100-file deploy with all-new blobs makes 200 storage calls. In finalize(), the uniqueHashes existence check is also sequential (for...of) rather than parallelized, adding sequential RTTs on the critical user-facing path.
findings.impact: Deploy and autosave latency is 2× what it needs to be for the storage phase. At 100 files with 8 concurrency slots this adds several seconds on S3. Finalize adds sequential latency proportional to the number of unique blobs.
findings.recommendation: Replace exists()+put() with an unconditional idempotent put() — on S3/MinIO a PUT of the same content-addressed key is a no-op in effect. Parallelize the finalize uniqueHashes existence check with Promise.all or bounded concurrency.
alternatives: Use S3 If-None-Match: * conditional PUT if the storage driver supports it.
Accept the current approach for local-storage deployments where exists() is cheap, and only optimize the S3 path.

findings.title: Guest email written to audit_log with no retention policy
findings.problem: The guest invite audit event writes the full email address into the audit_log.meta JSON column. The audit_log has no TTL, no retention prune, and no deletion mechanism — unlike usage_events which has a documented 90-day prune. The email accumulates indefinitely and is accessible to anyone with SELECT access to the audit table (SRE, backup recipients, support tooling).
findings.impact: Guest email addresses are retained indefinitely in the audit log, which is an GDPR/data-minimization concern for any deployment handling external guest invitees. The risk is proportional to the org size and the volume of guest invitations.
findings.recommendation: Replace meta: { email } with a pseudonymised reference, e.g. the allowlist entry ID or a truncated hash. If full email is required for compliance, implement a prune mechanism for audit_log consistent with the usage_events model (pruneBefore cutoff).
alternatives: Log only meta: { principalKind: 'guest', targetId: canvas.id } and rely on the allowlist table for email lookup.
Accept the current state for a trusted-org self-hosted deployment where the DB is operator-controlled, and document the retention gap.

findings.title: API key scan (looksLikeApiKey) absent from staged upload and draft write paths
findings.problem: The deploy engine scans each text file for canvas API key patterns (looksLikeApiKey) and emits a warning at deploy time (engine.ts line 127). This scan is absent from the stageBlob/stageFiles path and from draftService.writeFile. Files deployed via the staged upload API or edited in the in-browser editor do not produce the key-leak warning. Additionally, the engine's scan uses mime.contentType.startsWith('text/') which misses application/json and application/javascript files; isTextContentType() from mime.ts already covers these.
findings.impact: A canvas API key committed via the editor or the MCP add_files channel goes undetected until a full ZIP/folder deploy through the engine. The warning surface is inconsistent across all three ingestion paths, reducing the value of the §12.1.2 lint.
findings.recommendation: Apply looksLikeApiKey in stageOne (for text-mime blobs, after hash verification) and in draftService.writeFile (before or after the storage put). Also replace mime.contentType.startsWith('text/') with isTextContentType(mime.contentType) in engine.ts line 127 to cover JSON and JavaScript. Both changes are warn-only, non-blocking.
alternatives: Apply the scan centrally in finalize() when iterating manifest paths — one place, covers both staging channels.
Accept the gap given the scan is advisory only and owners control what they deploy.

findings.title: canvases.ts is a 900-line god module combining six distinct responsibilities — highest fan-in module in the server
findings.problem: The file combines canvas CRUD, gallery listing + facet queries, allowlist management, capability management, owner inventory summaries, and security-adjacent operations (password, API key regen). It exports 9 distinct types consumed directly by 23 non-test files across auth, canvas, deploy, mcp, routes, draft, upload, and http domains. Any change to the file — even adding an interface — touches all 23 dependents' type-checking and git blame history.
findings.impact: Growing this file increases cognitive load on every contributor and causes merge conflicts when multiple units touch gallery vs. allowlist vs. settings concurrently. The `revertPublicForOwner` bug (server-data-5) was likely harder to spot because the relevant constants (`CLEARED_PUBLICATION_FIELDS`) are hundreds of lines away in the same file.
findings.recommendation: Split into at minimum: `canvases-core.ts` (CRUD, status, password, API key), `canvases-gallery.ts` (gallery predicates, listGallery, listGalleryFacets, findCloneableTemplate), `canvases-allowlist.ts` (allowlist CRUD, isPrincipalAllowed). The gallery predicate is already a closure-scoped function; extracting it makes the split safe. Callers inject via typed interfaces, so the split is a refactor with no behavior change.
alternatives: Defer the split and add a linter rule capping file size as a forcing function
Split only the gallery queries (the most self-contained subset) as a first step

findings.title: pruneBeyond JSDoc block is displaced above deleteByCanvas — maintainers see the wrong documentation in their IDE
findings.problem: Two JSDoc blocks appear back-to-back before `deleteByCanvas`. TypeScript tooling associates a JSDoc with the immediately following declaration, so the detailed `pruneBeyond` concurrency explanation (lines 119-134) is attached to `deleteByCanvas`. `pruneBeyond` at line 144 receives only the `deleteByCanvas` docstring (lines 135-139) or nothing, depending on the tool. Additionally, the comment's phrase 'NOT from a snapshot' only applies to the live-pointer exclusion (the correlated subquery), not to the candidate list which IS a snapshot — the wording could mislead a future maintainer about the atomicity guarantees.
findings.impact: A maintainer hovering over `deleteByCanvas` sees the wrong, complex concurrency explanation; a maintainer reading `pruneBeyond` sees the wrong or no documentation. The misattributed comment about atomicity could lead to false confidence in the prune path's guarantees.
findings.recommendation: Move the `pruneBeyond` JSDoc to sit immediately above `async pruneBeyond(...)` at line 144, and the `deleteByCanvas` JSDoc immediately above `deleteByCanvas`. Clarify the comment to read: 'The candidate set is collected in a prior SELECT (a snapshot); the live-current exclusion is re-evaluated atomically inside the DELETE as a correlated subquery, so a concurrent rollback is still protected.'
alternatives: Reorder the functions so `pruneBeyond` appears before `deleteByCanvas` in the file (the JSDoc blocks then naturally fall above their methods)
Add a `// see pruneBeyond JSDoc above` cross-reference without moving code

findings.title: files-service.delete() silently swallows blob deletion failure after the DB row is already removed — orphaned blobs accumulate invisibly
findings.problem: `storage.delete(row.storageKey).catch(() => {})` silently discards any storage error after the DB row has already been removed. The caller receives `true` (success), the file is inaccessible (no DB row), but the storage blob is orphaned. There is no log, no metric, and the orphaned blob key no longer maps to any canvas row so the GC sweep cannot find it by normal means.
