findings.impact: Storage accumulates orphaned blobs silently over time with no operator visibility. In an S3 deployment this translates to unnoticed storage cost growth.
findings.recommendation: Replace `.catch(() => {})` with `.catch((err) => log.warn({ err, canvasId, storageKey: row.storageKey }, 'blob delete failed after row removal — orphaned blob'))`. The best-effort swallow is architecturally correct (the row is authoritative); the silence is not.
alternatives: Add a periodic orphan-scan that lists storage keys and cross-references them against known canvas/file rows (addresses the symptom, not the root cause)
Wrap the row delete and blob delete in a compensating transaction pattern (more complex, may be overkill for this surface)

findings.title: purge.ts: four sequential independent I/O awaits per canvas — straightforward Promise.all parallelization available
findings.problem: Inside the per-canvas purge loop, `versions.listByCanvas`, `drafts.getByCanvas`, `storage.list(blobPrefix)`, and `storage.list(screenshotPrefix)` are awaited sequentially. All four are independent reads. On S3, each `list` call involves a network round-trip; the sequential pattern multiplies latency per canvas by 4x.
findings.impact: Purge sweeps over many canvases take significantly longer than necessary, particularly with S3 storage where each list call is a network request.
findings.recommendation: Replace the four sequential awaits with `const [versions, draft, keys, shotKeys] = await Promise.all([deps.versions.listByCanvas(canvas.id), deps.drafts.getByCanvas(canvas.id), deps.storage.list(canvasBlobPrefix(canvas.id)), deps.storage.list(screenshotPrefix(canvas.id))]);`
alternatives: Defer until M10 load testing reveals this as a measurable bottleneck
Parallelize only the two storage.list calls (the DB reads are local and fast in SQLite deployments)

findings.title: session.touchExpiry() and users.upsert() fire unconditionally on every authenticated request — unnecessary write pressure on the hot path
findings.location: lines 58-65 (resolveUserId, called on every request); apps/server/src/auth/identity-mapping.ts lines 64-76
findings.problem: Every authenticated request issues at minimum two serial write round-trips before reaching any business logic: a full `INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` on the users table (upserting email/name/avatarUrl/lastSeenAt even when nothing changed) and a `UPDATE sessions SET expires_at` to roll the 14-day TTL forward. At moderate traffic these create sustained write pressure on both tables.
findings.impact: At 50-100 req/s on a single VPS (the M10 load-test target), this is 100-200 unnecessary write transactions per second. On SQLite (the default deployment) writes serialize under a write lock, directly reducing throughput for all concurrent requests.
findings.recommendation: Gate `touchExpiry` on elapsed time: if `expiresAt` is still more than 12 days away (TTL is 14 days), skip the write — ~90% of touches become no-ops. For users.upsert, check whether the identity fields differ from the cached user before issuing the UPDATE, or separate `lastSeenAt` into a time-gated `touchLastSeen()` call.
alternatives: Accept the current behavior and rely on SQLite WAL mode to amortize the writes (simplest, may be sufficient at single-VPS scale)
Move session and user updates to a background queue that batches them (more complex, higher payoff at scale)

findings.title: guestRepository and auditRepository have no repository-level unit tests — dual-dialect contract and key invariants are untested at this layer
findings.problem: `guestRepository` is exercised only through higher-level service/route tests. Repository-level invariants are untested: `markConsumed` single-use CAS atomicity (two concurrent magic-link clicks), `revokeAllForCanvas` atomicity (invite + session revocation), `createInvite` upsert idempotency. `auditRepository` has no dedicated test file — its dual-dialect behavior, ordering, and field mapping are not directly verified. Every other repository with meaningful write paths has a dedicated dual-dialect test file.
findings.impact: If `markConsumed`'s CAS predicate were accidentally widened (e.g. dropping the `state='pending'` guard), no existing test would catch it. The `auditRepository.recent` return type is also inferred as `any` (no explicit return type annotation), making caller type safety dependent on tests that don't exist.
findings.recommendation: Add `apps/server/src/db/repositories/guest.test.ts` covering both dialects via `DIALECTS`/`makeTestDb`: createInvite idempotency, markConsumed single-use, revokeInvite, revokeAllForCanvas, findLiveSessionByTokenHash excluding revoked/expired. Add `apps/server/src/db/repositories/audit.test.ts` covering: append inserts correct fields, recent returns newest-first, recent respects limit. Also add an explicit return type `Promise<AuditRow[]>` to `recent()`.
alternatives: Add integration tests at the service layer that exercise these paths end-to-end (less precise but faster to write)
Add only the guest.test.ts as the higher-risk gap, deferring audit.test.ts

findings.title: StorageError.code is an untyped string — no compile-time exhaustiveness for callers switching on error codes
findings.problem: `StorageError.code` is typed `string`. The three valid codes (`'not_found'`, `'delete_failed'`, `'invalid_key'`) are used as string literals at the three throw sites, but TypeScript cannot enforce exhaustiveness in any switch on `StorageError.code`, and a typo (`'notFound'`) silently falls through to the generic-error path.
findings.impact: A future driver or test double that uses a slightly different code string silently bypasses all intended error handling. As the number of codes grows this becomes increasingly fragile.
findings.recommendation: Add `export type StorageErrorCode = 'not_found' | 'delete_failed' | 'invalid_key'` and change the constructor signature to `constructor(message: string, public readonly code: StorageErrorCode)`. The three existing throw sites already use the exact literal strings; no call sites need changes.
alternatives: Keep as string but add a JSDoc enumerating the valid values (documentation only, no type enforcement)
Defer until a caller actually needs to switch on error codes

findings.title: KV limit constants are exported from a route file and imported by the admin config layer
findings.problem: KV_MAX_KEYS_SHARED and KV_MAX_KEYS_USER are exported from canvas-kv.ts and imported by apps/server/src/admin/config-fields.ts. This creates an upward dependency from the admin configuration layer into a route-handler file. Routes are a leaf layer; constants referenced from two distinct layers belong in a shared module.
findings.impact: If canvas-kv.ts is restructured or split, admin/config-fields.ts breaks. It also makes the dependency graph confusing for future maintainers.
findings.recommendation: Move the four KV limit constants (KV_MAX_VALUE_BYTES, KV_MAX_KEY_BYTES, KV_MAX_KEYS_SHARED, KV_MAX_KEYS_USER) to apps/server/src/canvas/kv-limits.ts. Both canvas-kv.ts and admin/config-fields.ts then import from a shared constants module with no upward dependency.
alternatives: Move only the two quota keys (KV_MAX_KEYS_SHARED, KV_MAX_KEYS_USER) that are referenced from admin, leaving the byte limits in canvas-kv.ts.

findings.title: managementRoutes JSDoc says 'Deploy routes added by U19' but they are already present
findings.problem: The JSDoc reads 'Deploy routes are added by U19.' The paste/zip/folder deploy endpoints are already present at lines 857–963 under the comment '--- Deploy entry points (UI calls these; the engine + result shape is U18/U19) ---'. A reader of the JSDoc would incorrectly conclude the deploy routes are not yet registered and might add them again or search elsewhere.
findings.impact: Developer confusion: a reader would waste time looking for deploy route registration that is already present, or might duplicate it.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the JSDoc: remove the 'added by U19' clause and describe what is actually mounted, e.g. 'Canvas lifecycle management API (§11.3), mounted at /api/canvases. Covers CRUD, settings, access, deploy, rollback, and lifecycle.'
alternatives: Simply delete the stale clause and leave the rest of the JSDoc intact.

findings.title: securityHeadersMiddleware docstring incorrectly claims all self-Response handlers call baseSecurityHeaders
findings.problem: The docstring states 'Self-Response handlers (canvas serve, file serving, SPA shell, draft preview, disabled page, 404) call baseSecurityHeaders on their own Headers ... The single source of truth so a new surface can't ship without them.' serve-sdk.ts and deploy-api.ts GET /:id/files both build raw Response objects without calling baseSecurityHeaders. The guarantee is false, which makes future audits misleading.
findings.impact: Future developers adding a new self-Response handler will read the docstring, assume the pattern is consistently enforced, and may not think to check whether their new handler also omits the call.
findings.recommendation: Fix the handlers (see server-http-5) and update the docstring to list the remaining exceptions explicitly. Or, once all handlers are fixed, the docstring's guarantee becomes accurate without editing.
alternatives: Add a linting check or test that scans for `new Response(` calls and asserts baseSecurityHeaders is called in the same scope.

findings.title: TOCTOU race on KV key-count limit allows exceeding per-scope quota under concurrency
findings.problem: overKeyLimit performs a non-atomic read-then-write: kv.get (existence check), then kv.countKeys, then the caller's kv.set or kv.increment are three separate DB round-trips. Two concurrent PUT requests for the same new key can both observe countKeys < limit before either write commits and both insert, exceeding the cap by 1 per concurrent pair. Node.js is single-threaded but event-loop concurrent: multiple awaited handlers interleave freely.
findings.impact: On a multi-tab or scripted canvas a user can exceed the configured key quota, violating the stated spec limit. On the trusted-org model this is a correctness gap rather than a security issue.
findings.recommendation: Push enforcement into the DB layer: have kv.set accept an optional maxKeys argument and enforce it inside a single transaction (e.g. a conditional INSERT that counts existing keys atomically). If a DB-level guard is not feasible immediately, document the known over-count risk with a comment and add a regression test.
alternatives: Use a per-canvas mutex (e.g. an in-memory Map of Promises) to serialize concurrent writes for the same canvas, accepting the single-process limitation.

findings.title: Hub and guest revocation failures silently swallowed with no logging
findings.problem: All calls to `deps.hub.revalidateCanvas()`, `deps.hub.dropGatedNonOwners()`, and `deps.guests.revokeAllForCanvas()` use empty `.catch(() => {})` blocks. A failure in guest revocation means live guest sessions persist after archive/delete/unpublish with no log emitted. Hub failures leave live sockets open despite the triggering settings change. With no logging, persistent failures in either are completely invisible.
findings.impact: A persistent bug or resource exhaustion in the realtime hub or guest revocation service would go undetected: guests would retain access after archive and hub subscribers would receive stale data — with no log trail to diagnose the issue.
findings.recommendation: Replace empty catches with a logged swallow: `.catch((err) => c.get('log')?.warn({ err, canvasId: cv.id }, 'hub: revalidateCanvas failed'))`. This preserves the fire-and-forget intent while making persistent failures observable.
alternatives: Emit a structured metric (counter) on failure rather than a log entry, if a metrics system is available.
For guest revocation specifically, promote to error level given the access-control implication.

findings.title: Draft preview catch-all swallows all errors with no logging
findings.problem: The servePreview handler wraps its entire body in a try/catch that converts any thrown error to a previewNotFound response with NO logging. A DB connection failure, a bug in injectOnPageEditor, or a type error in resolveAsset all produce the same plausible-looking 404 with no observable trace. An operator watching persistent 'not found' preview responses has no path to diagnosing the root cause.
findings.impact: A systematic failure in the draft preview pipeline (e.g. a storage driver error) would surface as silent 404s with no diagnostic signal, potentially delaying incident response.
findings.recommendation: Log before degrading: `} catch (err) { c.get('log')?.error({ err }, 'draft preview: unexpected error, returning not_found'); return previewNotFound(c); }`. Preserves the no-raw-500 guarantee while making failures diagnosable.
alternatives: Narrow the catch to expected error types (e.g. storage NotFoundError) and let genuinely unexpected errors propagate to the global error handler as 500s.

findings.title: PII: email addresses in audit log meta and admin AI-usage response
findings.location: lines 220–228 (GET /api/admin/ai-usage); also guest-invite.ts line 70, admin.ts line 433
findings.problem: The admin AI-usage endpoint returns ownerEmail correlated with canvasId and cost in a JSON response. The guest-invite and allowed_email_add audit actions write the raw email address to the meta column of the audit log. Audit logs are typically retained indefinitely and accessible to SRE/support beyond the population authorized to read user PII. The admin canvas list also returns owner.email in every row of paginated bulk responses.
findings.impact: Email addresses for potentially all users flow through bulk API responses and persist indefinitely in the audit trail, creating a GDPR/CCPA compliance surface and a data-minimization gap.
findings.recommendation: For the AI-usage endpoint: replace ownerEmail with ownerId (the admin can navigate by ID). For the admin canvas list: drop email from the owner projection, leaving id and name. For audit meta: store an opaque ID or hash instead of the raw email in guest_invite and allowed_email_add events.
alternatives: Add a data-retention policy that periodically scrubs PII from audit meta after a defined window.
Expose email only on a dedicated single-user detail endpoint (GET /api/admin/users/:id) rather than in bulk list responses.

findings.title: publicCanvas function is misleadingly named and the name persists in an export with no external callers
findings.problem: The JSDoc explicitly warns 'Misnamed "public" for historical reasons. It is NOT a public/shared projection' and documents owner-only fields including disabledReason. The function is exported but has no external importers. A future developer importing publicCanvas for a gallery or shared-link view would, despite the warning, risk serving owner-only fields like disabledReason to non-owners.
findings.impact: Low-probability but meaningful: a future module that reaches for this export by name could leak owner-only information to non-owners.
findings.recommendation: Rename to ownerCanvasView (or ownerCanvas) and drop the now-redundant 'misnamed' warning. All call sites are in management.ts, so the rename is a single-file change. Remove the export if no external caller is planned.
alternatives: Keep the name but add a lint rule or a comment in the export that prohibits importing this function outside management.ts.

findings.title: Rollback body uses an unsafe type-cast instead of Zod, unlike all sibling routes
findings.problem: Both rollback handlers use `(await c.req.json().catch(() => ({}))) as { version?: number }` with a `typeof body.version !== 'number'` guard. This allows floats (1.5), 0, and negative numbers to pass since `typeof 1.5 === 'number'` is true. All other mutating handlers in both files use `z.object({...}).safeParse(...)`. The inconsistency also means the error envelope uses `{ code: 'INVALID_PATH' }` on the management surface where `{ error: 'invalid_body' }` is the documented convention.
findings.impact: A caller passing `version: 1.5` or `version: -1` gets no validation error — the value silently finds no DB row and returns a misleading 404. The wrong error envelope (`code` vs `error`) requires dashboard/MCP clients to handle two shapes for the same surface.
findings.recommendation: Replace the cast with `z.object({ version: z.number().int().positive() }).safeParse(...)` in both files, consistent with the draft-api.ts restore handler. Return `{ error: 'invalid_body' }` on parse failure to match the management surface convention.
alternatives: Extract a shared `rollbackCanvas()` service helper called by both route files so the validation and logic are not duplicated.

findings.title: canvasView projection omits key settable fields, breaking read-your-writes for update_canvas
findings.problem: canvasView returns only id, slug, url, title, status, publicationState, currentVersionId, previewMode, hasPreview, and previewUrl. Fields that agents commonly need to confirm after calling update_canvas — access, description, hasPassword, galleryListed, galleryTemplatable, guestAiEnabled, guestAiCap, backendEnabled, spaFallback — are absent. An agent calling update_canvas { access: 'whole_org' } cannot confirm the change from the response; it must make a follow-up call. The HTTP PATCH /settings returns the full publicCanvas projection.
findings.impact: Agents must issue a second tool call (get_canvas) to confirm any settings write, making multi-step agent workflows more expensive and more fragile.
findings.recommendation: Extend canvasView to include access, description, hasPassword, galleryListed, galleryTemplatable, gallerySummary, galleryTags, guestAiEnabled, guestAiCap, sharedExpiresAt, spaFallback, backendEnabled, and disabledReason. Update the cv parameter type from the widened string fields to include these columns — they are already on the Canvas DB type.
alternatives: Add only the most frequently-written fields (access, hasPassword, galleryListed) as a partial improvement.
Defer until agents report confusion in production; the current behavior is functional even if verbose.

findings.title: server.ts is a 1021-line God Module covering 6 domains with 38 imports
findings.problem: server.ts registers 26 tools across 6 functional domains (identity, lifecycle, deploy, settings, sharing, usage) and imports from 11 internal subsystems. The draft-tools.ts extraction has already been applied as a pattern but the remaining 26 tools have not been similarly factored. Any change to a service interface creates a blast radius through this single file.
findings.impact: Merge conflicts are frequent on this file; per-module fan-out means that touching any one subsystem (e.g. the upload service interface) requires reasoning about the entire 1021-line file to assess impact.
findings.recommendation: Apply the draft-tools.ts register-function pattern to the five remaining thematic groups: registerDeployTools, registerLifecycleTools, registerSettingsTools, registerAccessTools, leaving whoami and get_canvas_usage in a thin top-level coordinator. Each module receives only the deps it needs, keeping files under ~200 lines.
alternatives: Extract only the deploy tools (the largest and most complex group) as a first step.
Accept the current structure until a concrete correctness bug is traced to the God Module, and defer the refactor.

findings.title: previewMode 'off' tool description says 'shows the generated cover' but server returns 404
findings.problem: The update_canvas tool's previewMode field description reads: 'off shows the generated cover'. The implementation in screenshots/serve.ts returns c.notFound() when previewMode=off; it is the dashboard client that falls back to a procedural GenerativeCover component. An MCP agent that reads this description and tries to fetch the preview URL when previewMode='off' will receive a 404, contradicting the tool description.
findings.impact: Agents that follow the tool description and expect a generated cover image URL to resolve when previewMode='off' will encounter unexpected 404s.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the description to: 'off disables the screenshot preview — the preview URL returns 404 and the dashboard falls back to a procedurally generated cover. Upload a custom image with set_canvas_preview.'
alternatives: Add a code comment only, without updating the tool description that the MCP SDK exposes to clients.

findings.title: Non-atomic SELECT-then-INSERT in clients.upsert can race under concurrent DCR registration
findings.problem: The Dynamic Client Registration upsert performs a SELECT followed by either an UPDATE or INSERT in two separate statements. Under concurrent registration for the same client_id, both requests can observe an empty result and both attempt an INSERT, causing one to fail with a unique-constraint violation that surfaces as an unhandled 500 rather than a proper DCR protocol error.
findings.impact: Under concurrent DCR registration (rare in trusted-org use, but possible in CI or automated agent setup), one request will fail with an unhandled 500 instead of a protocol-correct error response.
findings.recommendation: Replace the read-then-write with a single atomic INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE SET clientInfo = excluded.clientInfo via Drizzle's .onConflictDoUpdate(). This mirrors the pattern used in sessionsRepository and eliminates the race window.
alternatives: Catch the unique-constraint violation at the call site and retry as a less invasive fix.
Accept the current behavior as low-risk given the trusted-org deployment model and document the known gap.

findings.title: unpublish_canvas returns an informal error string instead of a stable CODE: message error code
findings.problem: When canvases.unpublish returns false, the tool returns fail('this canvas isn\'t published') — a bare message with no machine-readable prefix. Every other named failure in server.ts uses the CODE: message convention (NOT_ARCHIVED, SLUG_TAKEN, NOT_ACTIVE, CANNOT_UNPUBLISH in the HTTP route). The HTTP counterpart returns { code: 'CANNOT_UNPUBLISH', message: '...' }. An agent or test matching on error codes cannot distinguish this failure from other errors.
findings.impact: Agents cannot reliably detect and handle the 'already unpublished' condition without brittle substring matching on the error message.
findings.recommendation: Change line 527 to return fail('CANNOT_UNPUBLISH: this canvas isn\'t published') to match the CODE: message convention used throughout server.ts and mirror the HTTP route's CANNOT_UNPUBLISH code.
alternatives: Accept the current informal message if agents are not expected to branch on this error code.

findings.title: effectiveScreenshotsEnabled hits the DB on every get_canvas and list_canvases call with no caching
findings.problem: Every call to previewIds invokes resolvePreviewIds which calls deps.screenshotsEnabled(), which in production resolves to settingsSvc.effectiveScreenshotsEnabled() -> boolOverride(key) -> a DB SELECT on the settings table. This query fires on every get_canvas and list_canvases MCP tool call. The screenshots-enabled flag is an org-wide admin toggle that changes at most a few times in the lifetime of a deployment.
findings.impact: Every canvas list or get operation pays an extra DB round-trip for a value that almost never changes, adding unnecessary latency proportional to call frequency.
findings.recommendation: Cache effectiveScreenshotsEnabled() in the settings service with a short TTL (e.g. 30 seconds) using a simple in-process memoize. Alternatively, invalidate an in-memory flag explicitly when the admin changes the setting. This eliminates the per-request DB hit for the common case.
alternatives: Pass the screenshots-enabled flag as a startup-time config value rather than a DB-backed toggle, eliminating the runtime query entirely.
Accept the current behavior at the current trusted-org scale and defer caching until load tests surface the latency.

findings.title: Guest invitee email persisted indefinitely in audit log meta column
findings.problem: When a guest is invited via grant_access, the invitee's email address is written into the audit log's JSON meta column. The audit table is append-only with no TTL or retention policy. If a guest is later revoked or never accepts the invite, their email remains in the audit trail forever with no deletion path.
findings.impact: Guest email addresses accumulate indefinitely in the audit log, accessible to anyone with admin or database access, with no mechanism to honor deletion requests for former guests.
findings.recommendation: Replace the raw email in audit meta with a pseudonymous identifier (e.g. the allowlist entry id or a SHA-256 hash of the email). The action name 'guest_invite' carries the semantic meaning; the allowlist table already records the full email for operational purposes.
alternatives: Implement a data-retention policy with periodic redaction of audit meta for revoked/rejected guest records.
Accept the current behavior for the trusted-org use case and defer until a compliance requirement mandates change.

findings.title: draftViewFor in draft-tools.ts duplicates dirty-computation logic from draft-api.ts
findings.problem: The private draftViewFor helper replicates the same field projection and dirty computation as draftView + isDirty in draft-api.ts. Both produce the same output shape sorted by path with identical dirty logic. If either copy's dirty predicate drifts, the HTTP and MCP surfaces will disagree on which drafts are dirty with no compiler signal.
findings.impact: A future change to the dirty-detection logic in draft-api.ts that is not mirrored in draftViewFor will produce silent disagreement between the dashboard and MCP views of draft state.
findings.recommendation: Export a shared serializeDraftView(draft, liveManifest) helper from a shared module (e.g. draft/view.ts) and have both draftViewFor and draftView delegate to it. At minimum, export isDirty from draft-api.ts and import it in draft-tools.ts so the predicate has one definition.
alternatives: Export isDirty only (smaller change) to ensure the dirty predicate stays synchronized while leaving the projection code duplicated.
Accept the duplication and add a comment cross-referencing both files.

findings.title: canvasView and previewVisible accept status: string / previewMode: string instead of narrower domain types
findings.problem: canvasView accepts status: string and previewMode: string on its cv parameter, then casts status with as CanvasStatus to call publicationState. The narrower types CanvasStatus and PreviewMode already exist in the shared db package. The as CanvasStatus cast papers over a widened input type rather than enforcing the domain type at the boundary. The same widening applies to previewVisible in preview-ids.ts, which silently defaults to the auto branch for any unrecognized previewMode string.
findings.impact: A typo in a synthetic status override (e.g. { ...cv, status: 'Archived' }) passes the TypeScript compiler and reaches the as CanvasStatus cast at runtime with no error, silently producing wrong publicationState output.
findings.recommendation: Narrow the cv parameter to { status: CanvasStatus; previewMode: PreviewMode; ... } and remove the as CanvasStatus cast. For spread-override call sites like { ...cv, status: 'archived' as const }, TypeScript infers the literal correctly once the parameter is narrowed.
alternatives: Add a runtime assertion instead of narrowing the type, for a minimal-risk fix.
Accept the widened type and keep the cast, relying on call-site discipline.

findings.title: mailer.ts module JSDoc says 'Three drivers' — smtp is the undocumented fourth
findings.problem: The module-level JSDoc lists three drivers and the `canSend` JSDoc omits `smtp`. The `smtp` driver is fully implemented, wired in factory.ts, and has its own test file. A maintainer reading only this file will not know smtp exists and may add a fifth driver without looking for the fourth. The `canSend` description wrongly implies smtp always has `canSend: false`.
findings.impact: Misleads maintainers about available email transports; low probability of causing a bug but high probability of causing confusion.
findings.recommendation: Update the module JSDoc count to 'Four drivers' listing smtp. Update `canSend` JSDoc: 'True for `mailgun` and `smtp` when configured; also `log` (dev); false for `noop` or unconfigured drivers.'
alternatives: Add a single consolidated table of all drivers in the factory.ts JSDoc instead.

findings.title: hub Conn.sends JSDoc says 'per-user' rate limiting but it is per-connection
findings.problem: The JSDoc on `Conn.sends` reads 'Sliding-window publish timestamps for per-user rate limiting.' However, `sends` is a field on each `Conn` (connection), not aggregated across all connections for the same user. A user with two open tabs has two independent rate-limit windows and can publish up to 2 × MAX_MESSAGES_PER_MIN (200/min). The comment implies a global per-user limit.
findings.impact: Future developers adding cross-connection rate limiting may not realize the current limit is per-connection and can be bypassed by opening multiple tabs.
findings.recommendation: Change to: 'Sliding-window publish timestamps for per-connection rate limiting. A user with multiple connections has a separate window per connection.'
alternatives: Change the implementation to actually aggregate across connections for a given user.

findings.title: setupMailer switch: 'log' driver handled by implicit default, breaking exhaustiveness
findings.problem: The config schema defines four drivers (`log`, `smtp`, `mailgun`, `noop`). The switch has explicit cases for `smtp`, `mailgun`, and `noop`; `log` silently falls through to `default`. If a fifth driver is added to the config enum and the developer forgets a case, it silently uses the log driver (canSend=true, tokens in logs) instead of failing loudly.
findings.impact: Future driver additions risk silent fallback to the log driver, emitting credentials to logs in production.
findings.recommendation: Add `case 'log': return logMailer(log);` explicitly, then make `default` throw `new Error('unknown email driver')`. TypeScript will then enforce exhaustiveness at the call site.
alternatives: Add a compile-time exhaustive check using a never-type assertion in default.

findings.title: JSON.stringify(SEARCH_INDEX) called on every /docs/search-index.json request
findings.problem: `JSON.stringify(SEARCH_INDEX)` is called on every request to `/docs/search-index.json`. `SEARCH_INDEX` is a module-level constant that never changes after startup, so the serialization result is also constant. The `Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600` header helps browsers, but every cache miss triggers a fresh serialization of a ~50 KB object on the server.
findings.impact: Minor repeated CPU work per cache miss. Zero-risk to fix; notable only because the data never changes.
findings.recommendation: Pre-serialize once at module load: `const SEARCH_INDEX_JSON = JSON.stringify(SEARCH_INDEX);` and serve the cached string directly.
alternatives: Pre-generate the JSON file at build time and serve it as a static asset.

findings.title: buildSkillZip uses synchronous blocking I/O on first /skill.zip request
findings.problem: `buildSkillZip()` calls `readFileSync`, `readdirSync`, and `zipSync` — all synchronous, event-loop-blocking operations — on the first HTTP request to `/skill.zip`. Subsequent requests return the memoized result immediately, but the first request blocks all concurrent requests (health checks, canvas serves, API calls) for the duration of the blocking I/O and CPU work.
findings.impact: Event loop stall on the first `/skill.zip` request. Sub-millisecond for small skill files in practice, but architecturally inconsistent with the async I/O used elsewhere in the server.
findings.recommendation: Pre-build the skill zip at server startup (call `buildSkillZip()` synchronously from `docsRoutes()` at mount time, before any request arrives), or convert to async I/O using `readFile`/`readdir` and fflate's async `zip`.
alternatives: Accept the current behavior with a JSDoc note that the first request triggers blocking I/O — it is memoized and the files are small.

findings.title: Hub rate-limit sliding-window expiry path not tested
findings.location: hub.ts lines 163-171; rate_limit test at line 176
findings.problem: The existing rate-limit test only verifies the cap fires within a single time window. The `conn.sends.shift()` pruning loop (which lets connections recover after the 60s window) is never exercised. If the pruning logic regressed (e.g. off-by-one on the boundary), a user would be permanently rate-limited after hitting the cap once.
findings.impact: Pruning regressions go undetected; a user who hits the cap would be locked out permanently rather than recovering after one minute.
findings.recommendation: Add a test that sends `MAX_MESSAGES_PER_MIN` messages at `now`, confirms the cap fires on the next message, then sends a message at `now + RATE_WINDOW_MS + 1` and asserts it succeeds (no `RATE_LIMITED` error).
alternatives: Cover this in an integration test against a real WebSocket handler.

findings.title: Hub revalidateCanvas fail-closed path on resolveCanvas throwing not tested
findings.location: hub.ts lines 287-292; no test for throwing resolveCanvas
findings.problem: The test covering `revalidateCanvas` with `resolveCanvas` returning `null` (canvas deleted) exists, but the throwing-resolveCanvas path (transient DB error → drop all sockets with `CLOSE_UNAUTHORIZED` / `'revalidation_error'`) is not exercised. A regression where the `catch` block continues instead of dropping would leave stale grants alive.
findings.impact: The most important fail-closed branch is untested; regressions in the DB-error path would allow stale WebSocket grants to survive undetected.
findings.recommendation: Add a test where `resolveCanvas` is replaced with `async () => { throw new Error('db down') }` and assert all connected sockets are closed with `CLOSE_UNAUTHORIZED`.
alternatives: Cover via an integration test that injects a DB error mid-heartbeat.
